title: The Growth Spurt Riddle — Rohan’s Unstoppable Appetite
subtitle: A parent’s guide to puberty, appetite, and Type 1 Diabetes (Indian diet)

items.title: The story: Rohan’s unstoppable appetite
items.content: Twelve-year-old Rohan is growing like a weed — his school pants are suddenly two inches too short and his voice cracks mid-sentence. At 49 kg he is right in the middle of his pubertal growth spurt. But he has Type 1 Diabetes. His current regimen is 43 units total per day, split 6-4-4-4: 6 units of long-acting (basal) insulin at night, and 4 units of rapid-acting insulin before breakfast, lunch and dinner. Lately his appetite is bottomless — he finishes a full plate and asks, “Is there anything else? I’m still starving!” His parents, Sunita and Amit, are frightened: “If he eats more his sugars will skyrocket. Should we lock the kitchen and restrict his food? Or is this hunger a sign his diabetes is out of control?” Wisely, they decide to ask their care team before changing anything.
keyPoints: 12 years, 49 kg, T1D on 43 units/day (6-4-4-4).
Bottomless appetite during a pubertal growth spurt.
The right instinct: consult the care team before restricting food or changing doses.

items.title: Why is Rohan so hungry? The energy surge and the “puberty wall”
items.content: Two big shifts happen together in puberty. (1) The energy surge: building muscle, bone and adult height needs enormous fuel, so a bigger appetite is a normal, healthy biological drive — not greed. (2) The “puberty wall” of insulin resistance: surging growth hormone acts like a shield that makes cells temporarily resistant to insulin. So a pubertal child needs more insulin per kilogram than they did when younger. Rohan’s 43 units for 49 kg is about 0.88 U/kg — entirely standard — and it will often climb above 1.0 U/kg at peak puberty. If a child is growing well with stable sugars, a huge appetite is a demand for fuel to be matched with the right insulin, not something to punish.
keyPoints: Appetite rises to fuel rapid growth — a normal drive.
Growth hormone causes temporary insulin resistance (the “puberty wall”).
Insulin needs scale up: ~0.88 U/kg now, often >1.0 U/kg at peak puberty.

items.title: How the Insulin-to-Carb Ratio (ICR) lets Rohan eat for his growth
items.content: A fixed dose (exactly 4 units before every meal) stops working once appetite surges — an extra bowl of rice or an extra roti will spike him, eating less will crash him. The Insulin-to-Carbohydrate Ratio (ICR) gives him freedom: it says how many grams of carbohydrate are covered by 1 unit of rapid-acting insulin. Care teams often estimate a starting point with the “500 rule” — divide 500 by the Total Daily Dose. For Rohan, 500 ÷ 43 ≈ 11.6, which the team rounds to a practical 1 unit per 10–12 g of carbohydrate. The exact ratio is always set and reviewed by the diabetes team.
keyPoints: ICR = grams of carbohydrate covered by 1 unit of rapid insulin.
500 rule (starting estimate): 500 ÷ Total Daily Dose. For Rohan, 500 ÷ 43 ≈ 11.6.
Practical starting ratio ≈ 1:10 to 1:12 — confirmed and adjusted by the care team.

items.title: How to count carbs and dose for an Indian thali
items.content: Carbs come almost entirely from the grain, lentil, starchy-veg, fruit and milk parts of the meal — not from paneer, egg, chicken or fish (these are protein/fat and add little carbohydrate unless batter-fried or in a sweet/thickened gravy). Worked example at a dinner ICR of 1:10: a large pubertal meal of rice or roti (~45 g carbs) plus a katori of dal (~15 g) = ~60 g total. Dose = total carbs ÷ ICR = 60 ÷ 10 = 6 units. So instead of his old fixed 4 units, Rohan safely takes 6 units to match the bigger plate and keep post-meal sugars stable. Approximate carb values (confirm with your dietitian): 1 medium roti/chapati ≈ 15 g; 1 katori cooked rice ≈ 30–45 g; 1 katori dal ≈ 15 g; 1 katori rajma/chole ≈ 20 g; 1 idli ≈ 12 g; 1 plain dosa ≈ 25 g; 1 katori poha/upma ≈ 25 g; 1 cup milk ≈ 12 g; 1 banana ≈ 25 g. Plain paneer, egg, chicken, fish, dahi (unsweetened) ≈ 0–2 g.
keyPoints: Count carbs from grains, dals, starchy veg, fruit and milk; meat/paneer/egg add little.
Dose = total carbohydrate grams ÷ ICR.
Example: 60 g at 1:10 = 6 units (not the old fixed 4).
Keep a simple carb chart of your child’s usual foods; confirm values with your dietitian.

items.title: Why Rohan goes to bed at 110 but wakes at 210 (the Dawn Phenomenon)
items.content: Sunita and Amit noticed a frustrating pattern: Rohan sleeps at 10 PM with a lovely 110 mg/dL, eats nothing more, yet wakes at 6 AM consistently around 210 mg/dL. In deep sleep the body releases a surge of growth hormone and cortisol, mostly between 2 and 5 AM. Growth hormone shields against insulin and prompts the liver to pour out stored glucose — the Dawn Phenomenon. Rohan’s basal (long-acting) dose of 6 units was set before the growth spurt, so it is no longer strong enough to break through this early-morning wall. Critically, the fix is NOT to increase his pre-dinner rapid insulin: his dinner is fully digested by midnight, so extra dinner insulin would crash him into a dangerous low long before the dawn surge.
keyPoints: Growth hormone + cortisol surge 2–5 AM raise glucose overnight.
Pre-growth basal of 6 units is now too small to cover the surge.
Do NOT raise pre-dinner rapid insulin — that causes midnight hypoglycaemia.

items.title: How to investigate and fix early-morning highs safely
items.content: Gather data before changing anything. For three consecutive nights, log the bedtime number, a 3 AM number, and the waking number. Interpretation: if the 3 AM reading is already low, the morning high is a rebound and dinner/basal may be too much; if the 3 AM reading is normal but the waking number is high, it points to the dawn surge — and the team will usually adjust the long-acting (basal) insulin dose or its timing to cover the early morning, rather than touching the dinner bolus. Always make basal changes with your diabetes team.
keyPoints: Log bedtime, 3 AM and waking glucose for three nights running.
Normal 3 AM + high waking = dawn surge → adjust basal dose/timing.
Low 3 AM + high waking = rebound → reduce, don’t add, insulin. Decide with the team.

items.title: How to read a CGM: Time-in-Range and the trend arrows
items.content: A Continuous Glucose Monitor (CGM) is a GPS for diabetes — it shows not just where the sugar is now, but where it is heading. First, focus on Time-in-Range (TIR) over the 14-day trend, not a single scary number after a heavy meal: aim to keep glucose between 70–180 mg/dL for at least 70% of the day. Second, read the trend arrow before a meal or correction. Arrow straight UP (rising fast): give the mealtime insulin 15–20 minutes before eating to give it a head start. Arrow HORIZONTAL (steady): inject as he sits down to eat. Arrow straight DOWN (falling fast): let him eat a few bites first before injecting, to prevent a sudden crash.
keyPoints: Judge control by 14-day Time-in-Range (70–180 mg/dL ≥ 70% of the day), not one reading.
↑ rising: pre-bolus 15–20 min before eating.
→ steady: dose as he starts the meal.
↓ falling: eat a few bites first, then inject.

items.title: The traffic-light “when to worry” checklist
items.content: Sugars will swing during puberty; parents need to separate a growth-pattern change (simple dose tweak) from an emergency. GREEN (manage at home): sugar spikes after an extra-large meal but returns to normal within 3–4 hours — update the food log and discuss the ICR with your educator. YELLOW (high alert, investigate): glucose over 250 mg/dL twice in a row three hours apart while the child feels well — check for a blocked/dislodged pump site or faulty pen, give a calculated correction dose, check blood or urine ketones, and push sugar-free fluids. RED (emergency, go to ER): glucose persistently over 250 mg/dL WITH moderate-to-large ketones (>1.5 mmol/L), OR vomiting, severe abdominal pain, rapid deep (Kussmaul) breathing, or unusual drowsiness/confusion — this points to Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA); do not wait, go straight to the nearest emergency room.
keyPoints: GREEN: post-meal spike that settles in 3–4 h — adjust ICR, manage at home.
YELLOW: >250 mg/dL twice, feels well — check site/pen, correct, check ketones, hydrate.
RED: high sugar + large ketones, or vomiting/abdominal pain/Kussmaul breathing/drowsiness — DKA, go to ER now.

items.question: Why does a pubertal child with Type 1 Diabetes often need more insulin per kilogram than a younger child?
items.answer: Because puberty floods the body with growth hormone (and cortisol), which act as a temporary shield that makes cells resistant to insulin. To overcome that resistance and still move glucose into cells, the same child now needs a higher dose per kilogram than before. This is why a total daily dose that was right last year drifts upward through the growth spurt — frequently from under 1.0 U/kg to above it at the peak. It reflects normal pubertal physiology, not failing or ‘out of control’ diabetes.
keyPoints: Growth-hormone-driven insulin resistance is the cause.
Higher U/kg is expected and normal during peak puberty.
Rising needs are physiology, not a sign of poor control.

items.question: How should parents respond when a growing T1D child is constantly hungry but blood sugars are stable?
items.answer: Feed the growth, match the insulin. Let the child eat to satisfy genuine hunger with wholesome food, because puberty has high metabolic demand and restriction risks stunted growth, nutritional gaps, nocturnal hypoglycaemia and secretive eating. Then work with the care team to count the carbohydrates in each meal and use an Insulin-to-Carbohydrate Ratio so the pre-meal dose scales with what is actually eaten. Do not arbitrarily double doses or cut out carbohydrates — adjust insulin to the food, not the food to a fixed dose.
keyPoints: Allow healthy eating to fuel growth; don’t restrict a growing child.
Carb-count and dose with an ICR so insulin matches intake.
Avoid arbitrary dose doubling or zero-carb diets.

items.stem: Rohan (12 y, 49 kg, 43 units/day) is constantly hungry after his scheduled meals, but his glucose logs are stable and in target. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Strictly restrict his portions — high appetite in T1D always means sugars will become unmanageable if he eats more
Let him eat to satisfy his natural hunger, and work with the team to use insulin-to-carb ratios so his mealtime doses match his larger intake
Keep his food the same but immediately double his 6-4-4-4 doses to suppress his appetite
Switch him to a zero-carbohydrate diet so he can eat freely without needing rapid-acting insulin
items.explanation: Puberty is a non-negotiable period of high metabolic demand and growth-hormone-driven insulin resistance, so his total dose will naturally scale up. The correct approach is to allow wholesome food to fuel growth while adjusting pre-meal insulin to the carbohydrates eaten (ICR). Restriction (A) stunts growth and risks nocturnal hypoglycaemia; arbitrary doubling (C) risks severe unpredictable lows; a zero-carb diet (D) is unsustainable and dangerous, risking growth failure and DKA.

items.stem: Rohan’s CGM reads 260 mg/dL at 4 PM. He has a mild stomach ache, has vomited once, and his urine strip shows LARGE ketones. What is the correct action?
options: Wait 2 hours to see if his body clears the high sugar, and give a carbohydrate-heavy snack to settle his stomach
Assume the CGM is uncalibrated, stop checking for the day, and let him sleep it off
Take him immediately to the nearest emergency facility — high sugar with vomiting and large ketones signals impending DKA needing IV fluids and monitoring
Give another dose of his long-acting bedtime insulin now to bring the sugar down gradually
items.explanation: Vomiting plus high glucose plus moderate-to-large ketones is a definitive red flag for Diabetic Ketoacidosis: lacking effective insulin, the body burns fat and generates toxic ketones, and vomiting accelerates dehydration so it cannot be managed safely at home. Immediate hospital care with IV hydration and an insulin protocol is non-negotiable. Delaying (A), ignoring symptoms (B), or relying on slow long-acting insulin (D) all let DKA worsen dangerously.

items.stem: Rohan’s dinner ICR is set to 1:15 (1 unit per 15 g carbs). Dinner is 2 rotis (~30 g) + 1 katori rice (~45 g) + 1 katori rajma (~15 g) = 90 g carbohydrate. What mealtime dose matches this meal?
options: 4 units
6 units
9 units
15 units
items.explanation: Dose = total carbohydrate ÷ ICR = 90 ÷ 15 = 6 units. The ICR lets the dose flex with the size of the plate — a larger pubertal meal is safely covered by a larger, calculated dose rather than a fixed one.

items.stem: Rohan goes to bed at 110 mg/dL with no late snack but consistently wakes around 210 mg/dL. What is the safest next step?
options: Increase his pre-dinner rapid-acting insulin to bring the morning number down
Give a large bedtime snack so he doesn’t go low overnight
Log bedtime, 3 AM and waking glucose for three nights and review the basal dose/timing with the team
Stop his basal insulin to avoid overnight lows
items.explanation: Consistent morning highs after a normal bedtime number suggest the dawn phenomenon outrunning a basal dose set before the growth spurt. The safe move is to gather 3 AM data over three nights and let the team adjust the long-acting (basal) insulin. Increasing pre-dinner rapid insulin (A) causes midnight hypoglycaemia because dinner is already digested; a big snack (B) or stopping basal (D) worsen control.

items.stem: Just before dinner, Rohan’s CGM shows a glucose of 150 mg/dL with a trend arrow pointing straight up (rising fast). What is the best dosing strategy?
options: Skip the mealtime insulin entirely because he will burn off the rise while eating
Give the calculated mealtime insulin 15–20 minutes before he starts eating to give it a head start
Let him eat several bites first, then inject, to avoid a crash
Give double his usual dinner dose to catch the rapid rise
items.explanation: A rapidly rising arrow means pre-bolusing 15–20 minutes before the meal lets the rapid-acting insulin peak with the food and blunt the spike. (Letting him eat first is the strategy for a falling arrow; skipping insulin or doubling the dose are both unsafe.)

items.statement: During peak puberty, a child with Type 1 Diabetes may need more than 1.0 unit of insulin per kilogram per day.
items.explanation: True. Growth-hormone-driven insulin resistance commonly pushes requirements above 1.0 U/kg/day at the peak of puberty. Rohan’s current 0.88 U/kg is normal and will likely rise.

items.statement: Strictly restricting a growing child’s food is the safest way to control a big appetite in Type 1 Diabetes.
items.explanation: False. Restriction during peak growth risks stunted height, nutritional deficiency, nocturnal hypoglycaemia and disordered eating. The safe approach is to feed wholesome food and match insulin to carbohydrate intake.

items.statement: Vomiting with high blood sugar and large ketones can be safely managed at home with extra rapid-acting insulin.
items.explanation: False. That combination signals impending DKA. Vomiting drives dehydration and home subcutaneous insulin is not enough — it requires emergency care with IV fluids and a monitored insulin protocol.

items.assertion: Parents should not increase Rohan’s pre-dinner rapid-acting insulin to fix his 6 AM highs.
items.reason: His morning highs are driven by an early-morning growth-hormone surge, by which time his dinner insulin has already worn off, so extra dinner insulin would instead cause midnight hypoglycaemia.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason correctly explains the assertion. Because the dawn surge occurs hours after dinner insulin has peaked and faded, raising the dinner bolus only risks a midnight low without touching the morning high — which is why the basal dose, not the dinner bolus, is the right lever.

items.question: Rohan’s total daily dose is 43 units. Use the 500 rule to estimate a starting ICR, then calculate his dinner dose for a meal of 1 katori rice (45 g) plus 1 katori dal (15 g).
items.answer: 500 rule: 500 ÷ 43 ≈ 11.6 g per unit, which is rounded to a practical starting ratio of about 1:10 to 1:12. Taking 1:10 for dinner: total carbohydrate = 45 + 15 = 60 g, so dose = 60 ÷ 10 = 6 units. (At 1:12 it would be 60 ÷ 12 = 5 units.) The exact ratio and rounding are confirmed by the diabetes team.

items.title: Strengths, limitations, and the assumptions this story makes
items.content: Strength: it turns complex puberty/insulin physiology into actionable parent guidance with local Indian carb values and a clear safety triage. Limitation: the carb counts, the 500 rule and the ICR figures are illustrative averages that vary by individual, brand and cooking, and several sections assume CGM and laboratory access — none of it replaces the child’s diabetes team. Assumptions: a child on a stable basal-bolus regimen with reliable glucose logs, access to a diabetes team and laboratory, and (for some sections) a continuous glucose monitor.
keyPoints: Strength: complex physiology made practical, with local foods and safety triage.
Limitation: carb/ICR numbers are averages; assumes CGM/lab access; not a substitute for the care team.
Assumptions: stable basal-bolus regimen, reliable logs, and a reachable diabetes team.

items.assertion: A pubertal child with Type 1 Diabetes often requires a higher insulin dose per kilogram than a younger child.
items.reason: This is due to increased growth hormone and cortisol levels during puberty, which induce temporary insulin resistance.
items.explanation: Pubertal insulin resistance caused by growth hormone and cortisol explains the increased insulin requirements. This is a normal physiological adaptation, not indicative of poor diabetes control.

items.stem: How should parents manage a growing child with type 1 diabetes who is constantly hungry but has stable blood glucose levels?
options: A) Allow healthy eating to fuel growth, use insulin-to-carbohydrate ratio (ICR) for dosing, and avoid arbitrary insulin adjustments.
B) Restrict carbohydrate intake to prevent hyperglycemia and double insulin doses for meals.
C) Implement a zero-carbohydrate diet and adjust insulin based on hunger cues alone.
D) Discontinue insulin during periods of increased hunger to avoid hypoglycemia.
items.explanation: Allowing healthy eating to meet metabolic demands during growth, using ICR to match insulin to actual carbohydrate intake, and avoiding arbitrary dose changes prevents nutritional deficiencies, hypoglycemia, and disordered eating while maintaining glycemic control.

items.stem: Using the 500 rule, what is the appropriate dinner dose for a patient with a total daily dose of 43 units who is consuming a meal containing 60 g of carbohydrates (45 g rice + 15 g dal)?
options: A) 5 units
B) 6 units
C) 10 units
D) 15 units
items.explanation: The 500 rule calculates insulin-to-carbohydrate ratio (ICR) as 500 ÷ total daily dose (43) ≈ 11.6 g/unit, rounded to 1:10-1:12. Using 1:10, dinner dose = 60 g ÷ 10 = 6 units. 1:12 would yield 5 units, but 1:10 is the standard starting ratio.
